coredata.dc:description: Aqueous zinc-ion batteries (AZIBs) exhibit bright prospect in the next generation of large-scale energy storage, but the low specific energy and poor stability hinder their practical applications, and cathode materials with high specific capacity and excellent cycle performance are still lacking. In this work, chitosan-derived carbon dots (CCDs) are introduced into V2O5 through a simple hydrothermal method, resulting in CCDs@V2O5 nanobelts with unique one-dimensional structure and excellent electrochemical performance. The CCDs@V2O5 nanobelts have been employed as the cathode materials for AZIBs, demonstrating excellent rate performance and long-term cycling stability. At current density of 0.1 A·g−1, the specific capacity of the battery is 401.4 mAh·g−1. After cycling for 400 cycles at 1 A·g−1, the specific capacity remains at 285.3 mAh·g−1. After 2000 cycles at 4 A·g−1, the specific capacity still reaches 212.30 mAh·g−1 with capacity retention rate of 83.77 %. The introduction of CCDs effectively reduces the charge transfer impedance, and promoted the diffusion rate of Zn2+. Additionally, the insertion of Zn2+ in CCDs@V2O5 electrode is reversible by ex-situ XRD and XPS characterization. This work demonstrates that CCDs@V2O5 nanobelts have significant potential as cathode materials for AZIBs, and provides a new idea for the development of CCDs in the energy fields.

a) SEM of commercial V2O5 under at 1 μ m; b) SEM images of CCDs@V2O5 nanobelts at 300 nm; c) The TEM diagram of CCDs@V2O5 nanobelts at 20 nm; d) The distribution of CCDs on CCDs@V2O5 nanobelts. e) EDS spectra of CCDs@V2O5.

a) CV curves at 0.2 mv·s−1 and b) The discharge/charge curves of CCDs@V2O5 nanobelts at 0.1 A·g−1. c) Cyclic performance and Coulombic efficiency of CCDs@V2O5 and V2O5 at 1.0 A·g−1. d) Rate capabilities of CCDs@V2O5 and V2O5 electrodes at various current densities from 0.1 to 4.0 A·g−1. e) The discharge/charge curves of CCDs@V2O5 at different current density from 0.1 to 4.0 A·g−1. f) EIS curves of CCDs@V2O5 electrode in the initial state, after the 10th and 100th cycles. g) Long cycling performances of CCDs@V2O5 and V2O5 electrodes at 4.0 A·g−1.

a–c) Ex-situ XRD analysis of CCDs@V2O5 electrode. d–e) Ex-situ XPS spectra of V 2p and Zn 2p in different charge/discharge states at 0.1 A·g−1. f) Zn//CCDs@V2O5 battery internal schematic diagram. g) Zn2+ storage mechanism diagram of CCDs@V2O5 electrode.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Aqueous zinc-ion batteries (AZIBs) exhibit bright prospect in the next generation of large-scale energy storage, but the low specific energy and poor stability hinder their practical applications, and cathode materials with high specific capacity and excellent cycle performance are still lacking. In this work, chitosan-derived carbon dots (CCDs) are introduced into V2O5 through a simple hydrothermal method, resulting in CCDs@V2O5 nanobelts with unique one-dimensional structure and excellent electrochemical performance. The CCDs@V2O5 nanobelts have been employed as the cathode materials for AZIBs, demonstrating excellent rate performance and long-term cycling stability. At current density of 0.1 A·g−1, the specific capacity of the battery is 401.4 mAh·g−1. After cycling for 400 cycles at 1 A·g−1, the specific capacity remains at 285.3 mAh·g−1. After 2000 cycles at 4 A·g−1, the specific capacity still reaches 212.30 mAh·g−1 with capacity retention rate of 83.77 %. The introduction of CCDs effectively reduces the charge transfer impedance, and promoted the diffusion rate of Zn2+. Additionally, the insertion of Zn2+ in CCDs@V2O5 electrode is reversible by ex-situ XRD and XPS characterization. This work demonstrates that CCDs@V2O5 nanobelts have significant potential as cathode materials for AZIBs, and provides a new idea for the development of CCDs in the energy fields.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the continuous deepening of the global energy crisis and widespread attention to environmental protection, much efforts are devoted to advanced new sustainable energy technologies, committed to developing low-cost, environmentally friendly, safe, and high-performance rechargeable energy storage devices [1–4]. Lithium-ion batteries play a leading role in many electrochemical energy storage systems because of their high energy density and long cycle life [5–7]. However, the limited lithium resources, high cost, and toxic and unsafe organic electrolytes have severely limited the development of lithium-ion batteries [8,9]. Therefore, rechargeable metal batteries using aqueous electrolytes (such as Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+ and Al3+) can effectively solve the problem of increasingly scarce lithium resources and the safety hazards of organic electrolytes [10,11]. Among many water-based rechargeable metal ion batteries, Zn2+ with a radius similar to Li+ can carry more charges, and has a higher diffusion coefficient and lower binding energy required compared to Li+ embedded in the cathode crystal structure, enabling fast charging and discharging [12,13]. In addition, AZIBs are considered the most promising candidate for the next generation of large-scale energy storage due to their low cost, low redox potential (−0.76 V, relative to standard hydrogen electrode), high theoretical capacity (820 mAh·g−1) and high safety [14–16]. However, the formation of zinc dendrites and the dissolution of cathode materials during use limit the application and promotion of AZIBs [17]. The stability of the cathode materials needs to be improved due to the strong electrostatic interaction between Zn2+ and the host lattice, which leads to the structural collapse of the cathode materials during electron transfer [18]. Therefore, searching for new cathode materials that can promote the rapid diffusion and structural stability of Zn2+ is very important for the development of AZIBs [19].

The common cathode materials for AZIBs currently include manganese based oxides [20,21], Prussian blue analogues [22,23], and vanadium based oxides [24–26]. Among them, vanadium-based oxides, especially V2O5, are considered highly promising cathode materials due to their low cost, high theoretical specific capacity, and open crystal framework [27]. Layered V2O5 has a variable interlayer spacing, which can reduce volume changes during cycling and provide a diffusion channel for the insertion/extraction of Zn2+ [28]. Unfortunately, due to the slow ion diffusion reaction kinetics, low conductivity, and structural instability during cycling, the performance of V2O5 as the cathode materials for ZIBs is still poor [29,30]. Currently, several strategies have been proposed to improve the electrochemical performance of layered vanadium oxides, including pre-intercalated of metal cations [31,32], introduction of crystallization water [33], and composite with highly conductive materials [34,35]. Pre-intercalated of metal cations (such as Al3+ [36], Mn2+ [37], K+ [38], etc.) and introduction of crystallization water can expand the interlayer spacing of the material, accelerating the diffusion rate of Zn2+. However, the detachment of pre-intercalated ions has been found in some vanadium-based cathodes, resulting in unstable structures and a decrease in battery performance [39]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop new strategies to prepare vanadium oxide composite materials as the cathode of AZIBs.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a new type of “zero”-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, have gradually been discovered for their energy storage properties [40]. Considering green chemistry and practical applications, low-cost, pollution-free, and renewable biomass derived CDs are becoming increasingly popular [41]. In addition, elements such as N, S, and P in biomass can achieve self-doping of CDs, which helps to regulate the surface electronic structure [42]. For instance, Wu et al. have used strong alkali as a catalyst and biomass as raw materials to prepare fluorescent CDs through microwave hydrothermal method. These CDs have conjugated graphite core groups, which increase the capacitance of graphene oxide by 184 % [43]. Zhao and co-workers have prepared rGO/CDs materials with uniform CDs decoration using CDs and GO as raw materials with high specific capacity of 308 F·g−1 at a current density of 0.5 A·g−1 [44]. Combining CDs with vanadium-based oxide can greatly enhance electrochemical performance of cathode materials. Decorating CDS onto VO2 nanorods surface can decrease charge transfer impedance, exhibiting stable cycling performance, and delivering a specific capacity of 229 mAh·g−1 even after 2000 cycles at a current density of 4 A·g−1 [45]. Zhang et al. prepared V2O5/CDs composite with excellent rate performance and good cycling stability. When the current density is 4 A·g−1, the specific capacity of 175 mAh·g−1 is retained after 1500 cycles [46]. However, while the V2O5/CDs composite prepared through this one-step process can attach CDs onto V2O5, it results in the formation of carbon coating layer on the surface of V2O5 as well. Therefore, the effect of CDs on the structure and electrochemical performance of V2O5 has not been clearly clarified.

Herein, we report a simple hydrothermal method for the synthesis of one-dimensional CCDs@V2O5 nanobelts. CDs have been prepared using chitosan as a single carbon source using a low-temperature hydrothermal method under mild reaction conditions without the need for any strong acid or base catalysis. The introduction of “zero”-dimensional CDs has a regulatory effect on the growth of V2O5 nanobelts, achieving uniform adhesion of CCDs on the surface of V2O5 nanobelts. Meanwhile, the oxygen vacancies caused by N elements in CDs can weaken the interaction between the main structures, which helps promote the diffusion of Zn2+. The unique one-dimensional structure provides more transmission paths for electron transfer and accelerates the diffusion reaction kinetics. The CCDs@V2O5 nanobelts have been employed as cathode materials of AZIBs, exhibiting superior rate performance and excellent long-cycle stability. At current density of 0.1 A·g−1, the specific capacity of the AZIBs is 401.4 mAh·g−1. A high specific capacity of 212.3 mAh·g−1 at 4 A·g−1, and a high-capacity retention rate relative to the initial specific capacity of 83.77 % after 2000 cycles is achieved. The coupling engineering of CCDs with V2O5 provides a new approach for the further development of cathode materials for high-performance AZIBs.

The reagents used were directly used without further purification, including chitosan ((C6H11NO4)n, Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd., degree of deacetylation ≥95 %), and vanadium pentoxide (V2O5, Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd., 99 %).

CCDs were prepared through a one-step hydrothermal method. Firstly, 0.6 g of chitosan was dissolved in 60 mL of deionized water, stirred well, and sonicated for 10 min. Then the mixed solution was transferred to a 100 mL polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) lined high-pressure vessel and maintained it in an oven at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was filtered through a filter membrane to remove impurities. The resulting solution was evaporated to 30 mL and freeze-dried to obtain CCDs powder for further characterization.

The CCDs@V2O5 nanobelts were prepared through a one-step hydrothermal method. 0.364 g of V2O5 was dispersed in 30 mL of CCDs solution after rotary evaporation, stirred for 30 min until the solution was evenly dispersed. Then the mixed solution was transferred to PTFE lined high-pressure vessel, and maintained it in a 140 °C oven for 24 h. After cooling to room temperature, it was centrifugally washed with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol for four times to obtain dark green sediment. The sample was placed in a vacuum drying oven at 80 °C overnight to obtain CCDs@V2O5 nanobelts. For comparison, V2O5 was prepared by hydrothermal method without adding CCDs using a similar method.

The crystal structure of the sample was determined by Ultima IV X-ray diffractometer at Cu K with a scanning range of 10–80°α Radiation (λ = Tested at a voltage of 40 kV and a current of 40 mA at 1.5418 Å). The surface morphology and microstructure images of the samples were obtained using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, FEI Verios 460) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Talos F200S). The functional groups on the surface of CCDs and the elemental composition information of materials were measured to use X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha). The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy of materials was obtained using a Fourier spectrometer (FTIR, Thermo Fisher Nicolet IS10). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was conducted in a nitrogen atmosphere at a heating rate of 5 °C/min on the TG209F (TGA instrument) instrument to evaluate the weight loss of the sample. The specific surface area and pore size distribution of the samples were obtained using the 3Flex (Micromeritics, USA) specific surface and pore analyzer. The fluorescence spectrum (FL) of CCDs was recorded using the F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (ITACHI, Japan) at a voltage of 400 V.

The electrochemical performance was measured in button cell. CR2032 button batteries were assembled in room temperature air environment. The active substance (70 wt%), conductive carbon black (20 wt%) and polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF, 10 wt%) were mixed in 1-methyl-2-pyrrolidinone (NMP) solvent. After mixing evenly, the slurry was coated on a circular carbon cloth with a diameter of 12 mm and maintained in a vacuum drying oven at 80 °C for 12 h to remove NMP. The loading mass of active substance was 1.8–2.2 mg. Zinc foil was used as the negative electrode of the battery, glass fiber (GF/A) was used as the separator, and 3 M Zn(CF3SO3)2 aqueous solution was used as the electrolyte. At room temperature, the cycling performance and rate capacity of the battery were tested by NEWARE Battery Test System (CT-4008Tn-5V/50 mA-HWX, Shenzhen, China), and the test range of voltage was 0.2–1.4 V. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) testing was performed on a Chi760e electrochemical workstation. EIS was measured at open circuit voltage with a frequency range of 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz. The constant current uninterrupted titration (GITT) technology was used to analyze the ion diffusion coefficient of the material on the NEWARE Battery Test System.

CCDs@V2O5 nanobelts have been synthesized by a facile hydrothermal method. As shown in Scheme 1 , CCDs are introduced into V2O5 using V2O5 as the precursor. The preparation of CCDs does not need to add any strong acid or base as catalyst, and employs chitosan as carbon precursor, directly through a simple one-step hydrothermal carbonization, which is in line with the current theme of green chemistry development. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of CCDs are shown in Fig. S1. CCDs are monodispersed nanoparticles with great dispersion and an average particle size of 3.08 nm. The lattice spacing of 0.21 nm can be observed by high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (insert in Fig. S1). Fig. S2 shows the FL spectrum of CCDs. The characteristic peaks of these spectra gradually shift to longer wavelengths. The maximum fluorescence excitation peak of CCDs is 400 nm, and the emission peak is 485 nm. When the excitation wavelength increases from 280 nm to 480 nm, the emission peak intensity of CCDs first increases and then decreases, and the peak red shifts, indicating that they have excitation dependence. This excitation dependence is mainly attributed to the existence of complex carbon core and surface defects in the CCDs [47]. The composition of functional groups on the surface of CCDs has been analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. S3. In the survey spectrum of CCDs (Fig. S3a), three main peaks of C 1s, N 1s and O 1s can be detected, indicating that the prepared CCDs are rich in nitrogen-containing and oxygen-containing groups. The C 1s energy spectrum (Fig. S3b) can be divided into three peaks at 284.8 eV, 285.9 eV into three peaks at 284.8 eV, 285.9 eV which are respectively attributed to C=C/C−C bond, C−O/C−N bond and C=O/C=N bond [48]. The three fitted peaks in the O 1s energy spectrum (Fig. S3c) are C=O/O−C=O, C−O−C and C−OH bonds with energies of 531.1 eV, 532.6 eV and 533.3 eV, respectively. Among them, the C−O−C bond is the main oxygen-containing group of CCDs. The peaks of N 1s energy spectrum (Fig. S3d) at 398.5 eV, 399.4 eV, 400.6 eV and 402.3 eV can be divided into pyridine nitrogen (C−N=C), pyrrole nitrogen (C−N−C), graphite nitrogen (N−C3) and nitrogen oxide (C−N−O) [49]. Among them, C−N−C bond is the main nitrogen-containing group of CCDs.

The phase structure of samples has been tested by X-ray diffraction (XRD). As shown in Fig. 1a, the diffraction peaks at 15.3°, 25.8°, 31.0°, 33.1°, 34.3°, 47.1°, 50.2°, 61.3° and 71.1° are respectively (200), (110), (301), (111), (310), (600), (020), (321) and (702) crystal planes, corresponding to the diffraction peaks of standard card V2O5 (JCPDS 41-1426) [50]. Compared with V2O5, the characteristic peaks of the (001) and (020) crystal planes of CCDs@V2O5 nanobelts shifts gradually to the lower degree. According to the Bragg formula ( 2 dsin θ = nλ ), the d-spacing of the (110) face increases from 0.34 nm to 0.35 nm; the d-spacing of the (020) face is enlarged from 0.18 nm to 0.20 nm, indicating that the introduction of CCDs enlarges the spacing between adjacent VO layers. The diffraction peak of CCDs@V2O5 nanobelts is relatively broad and shows low crystallinity, while the diffraction peak of V2O5 shows high crystallinity. In addition, CCDs@V2O5 shows a wide diffraction peak at 28.2°, which proves the presence of CCDs in the nanobelts [46].

The Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) of CCDs, V2O5 and CCDs@V2O5 are shown in Fig. 1b. As for CCDs, the typical peaks at 3434 cm−1, 1600 cm−1 and 1450 cm−1 represent the bending vibration of NH, CC and -COOH, and the stretching vibration at 1360 cm−1 is attributed to CO. For V2O5, the bending vibration of VO and the symmetric stretching of V-O-V correspond to peaks near 1010 cm−1 and 540 cm−1 in the spectrum, respectively. However, for CCDs@V2O5, CC at 1600 cm−1 and CO at 1360 cm−1 confirm the presence of CCDs. The valence states of CCDs@V2O5 nanobelts have been analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). In the XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 1c), four elements V, O, C and N can be observed, indicating that the N element has been successfully doped into the material. Among them, the N element comes from the amino group in CCDs. The three fitted peaks in the O 1s energy spectrum (Fig. 1d) are attributed to the H2O, OH− and O2− bonds, with energies of 533.3 eV, 531.7 eV, and 529.7 eV, respectively [46]. The two peaks at 517.1 eV and 515.6 eV in the V 2p3/2 energy spectrum (Fig. 1e) correspond to V5+ and V4+ respectively. This is due to the formation of oxygen vacancies in the hybridization, resulting in the formation of V4+. In addition, the ion radius of V4+ in the coordination bond is larger than that of V5+, which is conducive to the expansion of lattice spacing and can improve the reversibility of Zn2+ insertion and extraction [33]. The C 1s (Fig. 1f) spectrum can be divided into three peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.5 eV and 288.9 eV, which are attributed to CC, CO and CO bonds, respectively. The three main peaks in the N 1s (Fig. 1g) spectrum at 399.4 eV, 400.2 eV, and 401.2 eV are attributed to the C-N=C, C-N-C, and N−(C3) bonds [43]. The oxygen vacancy caused by N doping weakens the interaction between Zn2+ and the main structure, which is conducive to the diffusion of Zn2+ [37]. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) has been performed on the samples to evaluate their weight loss in the air stream heated at a rate of 5 °C·min−1. As shown in Fig. 1h, V2O5 exhibits hardly any change in its weight during the testing process. For CCDs@V2O5 nanobelts, the loss is about 2.39 % at 150 °C, corresponding to the loss of physical moisture. The weight loss from 200 to 600 °C corresponds to the decomposition of CCDs [43], and the mass fraction of CCDs in the nanobelts is determined to be 7.92 wt%. The specific surface area and pore size distribution of the sample has been studied. The N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of V2O5 and CCDs@V2O5 are shown in Fig. 1i. The adsorption and desorption branches of CCDs@V2O5 are separated and exhibit a typical type IV adsorption hysteresis, which is due to the presence of mesoporous structure. The BET surface area of CCDs@V2O5 is 33.0 m2 g−1, while the BET surface area of V2O5 is only 3.67 m2 g−1, indicating that the introduction of CCDs has expanded the specific surface area of V2O5. The increased specific surface area of V2O5 enhances the interface contact between the electrode and electrolyte, thereby reducing the diffusion path for Zn2+. The pore size distribution curve of the sample is shown in Fig. S4. The pores of CCDs@V2O5 are mainly composed of mesopores with a central diameter of 2–50 nm and a small amount of macropores with a diameter of 50–63 nm. These pore structures also provide more active sites for Zn2+ transport, which makes CCDs@V2O5 electrode have better electrochemical performance.

The morphology and structure of the samples have been obtained by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fig. 2a shows the surface morphology of V2O5, whose crystals have a layered structure with extremely high crystallinity. Fig. 2b shows the surface morphology of CCDs@V2O5 nanobelts at 300 nm. It can be observed that the sample is a uniform nanobelt with a length of 100–500 nm and a width of 50–100 nm (Fig. S5). The introduction of CCDs into V2O5 resulted in the formation of unique one-dimensional structured CCDs@V2O5 nanobelts, indicating that CCDs can promote the formation of nanobelts as structure directing agent [51]. The distribution directions of nanobelts are different, and the multi-dimensional charge transfer paths along the radial and axial directions are conducive to electron transfer [30]. The TEM image in Fig. 2c reveals the morphology of CCDs@V2O5 nanobelts at 20 nm. Moreover, CCDs attached to CCDs@V2O5 nanobelts has been observed by high resolution TEM (HRTEM). According to the analysis of the high resolution (HR) TEM image (Fig. 2d), the measured lattice spacing of 0.57 nm corresponds to the (200) crystal plane of the nanobelts, while the lattice spacing of 0.21 nm corresponds to the (100) diffraction plane of the CCDs. The color of CCDs is dark and evenly distributed in the nanobelts. Furthermore, energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) element mapping results are shown in Fig. 2e, indicating that V, O, C and N elements are evenly distributed throughout the nanobelts.

In order to evaluate the electrochemical performance of CCDs@V2O5 nanobelts as cathode materials for AZIBs, CR2032 button battery has been assembled by using metal zinc foil as negative electrode and 3 M Zn(CF3SO3)2 aqueous solution as electrolyte. Fig. 3a shows the initial three continuous cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves of CCDs@V2O5 cathode at a scan rate of 0.2 mV·s−1. These curves are roughly overlapped after the initial cycle, indicating that CCDs@V2O5 electrode has structural stability and high reversibility during the charge-discharge process. There are two pairs of redox peaks of about 0.91/1.01 V and 0.53/0.60 V correspond to the redox reactions of V5+/V4+ and V4+/V3+, respectively [52]. It can be observed that the redox peak is asymmetric in CV curves (Fig. 3a). This may be due to the different kinetic rates of Zn2+ insertion and extraction on the electrode surface during redox reaction. The mismatch in electrochemical reaction kinetics can lead to unequal potential windows and asymmetric current responses during charging and discharging processes [53]. In addition, during the initial charge-discharge processes, some irreversible phase transformations occur within the electrode materials, which also influence the shape of the CV curves. Fig. 3b shows the constant current charge-discharge curve of the battery in the first 3 cycles at 0.1 A·g−1. The initial voltage of the battery is about 1.12 V. The CCDs@V2O5 electrode with the first discharge shows two discharge platforms stable at about 0.97 V and 0.44 V during the discharge process, and two charging platforms stable at about 0.45 V and 1.03 V during the charging process, which is consistent with the CV Curve. The cycle performance of CCDs@V2O5 electrode and V2O5 electrode at 1.0 A·g−1 is shown in Fig. 3c. In the initial 20 cycles, the capacity of the battery decreases rapidly, which may be due to the residue of “dead zinc” in the electrode material [20]. After that, the battery cycle remains stable, with a capacity of 285.3 mAh·g−1 after 400 cycles, while the capacity of V2O5 electrode after 400 cycles is very low.

The rate performance is a pivotal indicator for evaluating the large-scale preparation of batteries. Fig. 3d shows the rate performance of V2O5 and CCDs@V2O5 electrodes at different current densities. The discharge capacities of CCDs@V2O5 electrode at 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.8, 1.0, 2.0, and 4.0 A·g−1 are 401.4, 366.8, 342.8, 310.1, 295.9, 252.6, and 194.9 mAh·g−1, respectively. When the current density returns to 0.1 A·g−1, the discharge capacity of CCDs@V2O5 electrode can still reach 371.8 mAh·g−1, which is equivalent to 93 % of the initial capacity, indicating that CCDs@V2O5 electrode has excellent rate performance and rate reversibility. Compared with CCDs@V2O5 electrode, V2O5 shows poor rate performance, which indicates that the introduction of CCDs greatly improves the rate performance of the battery. Moreover, the Coulombic efficiency exhibits instability during each increase in current, which may be due to the limitation of electrochemical kinetics of the material under the transition of different current densities. With the increase of current density, the kinetic limitations on ion transport and charge transfer result in the incomplete release of a portion of charges at high current density, thereby affecting the Coulombic efficiency [54]. During the following charge-discharge process, the Coulombic efficiency returns to normal.

Fig. 3e shows the constant current charge discharge curves from 0.1 A·g−1 to 4.0 A·g−1 at different current densities. During the discharge and charging cycles, the voltage plateau is well maintained, and all charge/discharge curves exhibit similar shapes and obvious voltage plateau. When the current density is high, the battery can achieve a fast charging and discharging process, indicating that CCDs@V2O5 electrode has fast charge transfer kinetics. Additionally, the power density and energy density are calculated for the CCDs@V2O5 electrode and compared with other vanadium-based materials such as FeVO4 [55], VS2 [56], Ba1.2V6O16·3H2O [57], and VS4 [58]. As shown in Fig. S6, at a power density of 135.3 W·kg−1, based on active material mass, the CCDs@V2O5 electrode achieved an energy density of 248.08 Wh·kg−1. Even at a high-power density of 2650 W·kg−1, the CCDs@V2O5 cathode could still provide 125.14 Wh·kg−1.

The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of the battery has been measured in the initial, 10th and 100th discharge states, as shown in Fig. 3f. The equivalent circuit model of Nyquist diagram consists of internal resistance (Rs), constant phase element (CPE1), charge transfer resistance (Rct) and Warburg impedance (W1). Rs represents ohmic resistance, while Rct represents charge transfer resistance between the electrolyte and electrode material interface contact, and W1 is attributed to the diffusion of Zn2+ in the electrode [39]. The Nyquist plot consists of a semicircle and a straight line, representing the high-frequency and low-frequency regions of the EIS spectrum, respectively. In the high-frequency region (semicircle), the electrochemical process of the electrode is mainly kinetic, and the transfer resistance (Rct) can be obtained by calculating the radius of the semicircle. The Rct of the undischarged battery is 104.2 Ω, while after the 10th discharge, the Rct is only 5.5 Ω, and after the 100th discharge, the Rct is 31.65 Ω. This is because the activation of the cathode materials provides more charge transfer channels [34]. However, the initial electrode resistance of V2O5 has been measured to be 571.6 Ω (Fig. S7). These results demonstrate that CCDs can effectively reduce charge transfer impedance, and accelerate the reaction kinetics of electrode.

To assess the cycling stability of CCDs@V2O5 electrode at high current density, a long-term cyclic test has been conducted on the battery at 4 A·g−1 (Fig. 3g). In the first cycle, an initial discharge specific capacity of 253.4 mAh·g−1 with a Coulombic efficiency close to 100 % can be obtained. After 1000 cycles, the retention rate of CCDs@V2O5 electrode relative to the initial discharge specific capacity is 88.82 %. Moreover, after 2000 cycles, the specific discharge capacity is 212.30 mAh·g−1, and the capacity retention rate is still 83.77 %, which is significantly better than pure V2O5 electrode (with a capacity of only 49.21 mAh·g−1 after 2000 cycles) and other advanced vanadium-based materials reported recently (Table S1). These results indicate that CCDs@V2O5 nanobelts have higher specific capacity and longer cycling stability compared to other electrodes. Fig. S8 depicts the galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) curves of the CCDs@V2O5 nanobelts at the current density of 4 A·g−1 for different cycle periods. With the increase of the number of cycles, the specific capacity gradually decreases. The specific capacity of the materials decays rapidly in the initial 50 cycles, which is mainly due to the irreversible phase transition of Zn2+ in the initial cycle, and some Zn2+ remains in the cathode materials [59]. In the subsequent cycle, the storage performance of electrode materials tends to be stable.

To further investigate the electrochemical kinetics of CCDs@V2O5 electrode, CV curves have been tested at different scanning speeds (0.4–2.0 mV·s−1) under a voltage window of 0.2–1.4 V, as shown in Fig. 4a. As the scanning rate increases, the current density of characteristic peaks increases, and these curves show similar shapes. Based on these scanning data, the capacitance effect can be analyzed using the linear relationship in the equation, and the relationship between the obtained CV peak current (i) and scanning rate follows the power-law formula (1) [60,61]:
(1)
I = a
v b
where a and b are adjustable parameters, and v is the scanning rate. The magnitude of the b values reflects the charge storage dynamics of the electrode. If the b values approach 0.5, the diffusion-controlled process dominates; while the values of b are close to 1, then the pseudo capacitance control process dominates. Here, we obtain the b values by fitting the curve between logi and logv . Therefore, the power law formula can be rearranged into Eq. (2).
(2)
log i = b log v + log a

The b values of peaks 1, 2, 3, and 4 are calculated to be 0.75, 0.90, 0.78, and 0.88, respectively (Fig. 4b), indicating that the capacity of the CCDs@V2O5 electrode is generated by the synergistic effect of pseudocapacitance control and ion diffusion process. In addition, at a specific voltage, the relative contributions of capacitance contribution (k1v) and diffusion contribution (k2v) can be quantitatively determined by Eq. (3) as below [60,61]:
(3)
I =

where
k 1
and
k 2
are adjustable parameters. Fig. 4c shows the CV curves fitted by the CCDs@V2O5 electrode at a scanning rate of 2.0 mV·s−1. The shaded area represents a capacitance contribution of 74.13 % in the total Zn2+ storage capacity. Fig. 4d shows the contribution ratio of pseudocapacitance control capacity and diffusion control capacity percentage at different scanning rates. When the scanning rate increases from 0.4 mV·s−1 to 2.0 mV·s−1, the capacitance contribution gradually increases from 56.88 % to 74.13 % (Fig. S9), indicating that capacitance control dominates the Zn2+ storage process. This can be attributed to the unique one-dimensional morphology of CCDs@V2O5 nanobelts [33]. The constant current intermittent titration technique (GITT) has been used to detect the diffusion coefficient of Zn2+ in CCDs@V2O5 electrode. The assembled battery is discharged or charged at a current density of 50 mA·g−1 for 30 min, and then relaxed for 1 h to balance the voltage. The procedure has been applied repeatedly throughout the charge and discharge process until the cut-off voltage is reached.

Fig. 4e shows GITT testing curves of the CCDs@V2O5 electrode, and the corresponding Zn2+ diffusion coefficient (Fig. 4f) is calculated according to formula (4) [62]:

of CCDs@V2O5 electrode in the Zn2+ insertion/extraction process is between 10−9–10−10 cm2 s−1, indicating that the

of charging process. The result shows that CCDs@V2O5 electrode exhibits great structural stability during Zn2+ insertion and extraction processes [63].

To further explore the structural changes and zinc storage mechanism of the material during constant current charging and discharging, the CCDs@V2O5 electrode has been tested by ex-situ XRD (Fig. 5a). Fig. 5b and c show the ex-situ XRD analysis of the CCDs@V2O5 electrode. In the whole charge-discharge process, the structural characteristic peaks of V2O5 remain basically unchanged, which indicates that the main structure of V2O5 keeps stable. The diffraction peak at 25.5° corresponds to the (110) crystal plane of CCDs@V2O5 nanobelts. When the discharge reaches 0.2 V, the diffraction peak moves to a low angle, which indicates that Zn2+ is gradually inserted into CCDs@V2O5, and the interlayer spacing of the nanobelts becomes larger. When charged to 1.4 V, due to the extraction of Zn2+, the layer spacing becomes smaller, and the diffraction peak returns to the original Angle, which indicates that the insertion and extraction of Zn2+ have great reversibility [64]. It is worth noting that during the discharge to 0.2 V, a new peak appeared at 33.14°. This is due to the co-insertion of Zn2+ and H2O, the structure of the CCDs@V2O5 electrode changes to form ZnxV2O5·nH2O [65]. In the ensuing charging process, the characteristic peaks gradually weaken and then disappear.

Fig. S10 shows the ex-situ XPS of CCDs@V2O5 electrode in initial state discharged to 0.2 V and charged to 1.4 V. The electrode does not have the presence of Zn in its initial state (Fig. 5d). When discharged to 0.2 V, two obvious Zn signals are detected at 1023 eV and 1045 eV, indicating that Zn2+ is inserted into CCDs@V2O5 layer. When charged to 1.4 V, the peak strength becomes weak, indicating that most of the insertion/extraction of Zn2+ is reversible, but a small amount of Zinc-based salt remains on the surface of the CCDs@V2O5 electrode [32]. Fig. 5e shows the V 2p XPS spectrum of the CCDs@V2O5 electrode. In the initial state, V has V4+ and V5+ valence states. The peak of 516.17 eV belongs to the V4+, and the peak of 517.72 eV belongs to the V5+. After discharge to 0.2 V, the proportion of V5+ decreases and the proportion of V4+ increases due to the insertion of Zn2+ into the CCDs@V2O5 electrode. When charge to 1.4 V, the proportion of V5+ increases and the proportion of V4+ decreases. This suggests that reversible redox reactions involving V element occur during the insertion and extraction processes of Zn2+ [66]. Fig. S11 shows the O 1s XPS spectra of CCDs@V2O5 electrode under different charging and discharging states. The O 1s spectrum can be divided into three peaks at 530.2 eV, 532.3 eV and 533.2 eV, belonging to O2−, OH− and H2O. The O 1s peak signal corresponding to H2O is enhanced during the discharging process of the CCDs@V2O5 electrode and almost unchanged during the next charging process, indicating that water molecules are embedded in the matrix and some water molecules are retained [46]. Moreover, the insertion of water molecules can improve the stability of the main structure and provide electrostatic shielding for Zn2+, which is beneficial to the rapid diffusion of Zn2+ [67]. Based on the above test results, the insertion/extraction mechanism of Zn2+ in Zn//CCDs@V2O5 battery is shown in Fig. 5f, where Zn and Zn2+ are converted back and forth through the membrane during charging/discharging processes. The schematic illustration of Zn2+ storage mechanism of CCDs@V2O5 nanobelts is presented in Fig. 5g. When the battery is discharged for the first time, Zn2+ and some water molecules are inserted into the CCDs@V2O5 nanobelts; when the battery is charged to 1.4 V, Zn2+ will be removed from the CCDs@V2O5 nanobelts, but it is not completely released, and some Zinc-based salt will still remain in the cathode materials.

In summary, this work introduces CCDs into V2O5 through a simple hydrothermal method. The introduction of “zero”-dimensional CDs promotes the formation of V2O5 nanobelts. The introduction of CCDs effectively reduces the charge transfer impedance. The unique one-dimensional structure of nanobelts can effectively shorten the diffusion path of Zn2+. The doping of N element weakens the interaction between the main structures, which is beneficial for the insertion and extraction of Zn2+. As a cathode material for AZIBs, the CCDs@V2O5 electrode exhibits a discharge capacity of 401.4 mAh·g−1 at 0.1 A·g−1 and superior rate capability. At a high current density of 4 A·g−1, the specific discharge capacity is still 212.30 mAh·g−1 after 2000 cycles with the capacity retention rate is 83.77 %, demonstrating excellent long-term cycling stability. Therefore, this work proves that CCDs@V2O5 nanobelts can be one of the candidate materials for the cathode of AZIBs, which provides a new path for the development and application of CDs in the energy field.
